verdict_reasoning: EREG is a secreted EGF-family ligand canonically described as a peptide hormone. However, EGF-family ligands including EREG are typically synthesized as membrane-anchored precursors (single-pass TM proteins) before ectodomain shedding by sheddases releases the soluble active ligand. The membrane-anchored precursor stage qualifies for surface accessibility via TM topology, even though the dominant biological form detected in circulation and in most assays is the shed soluble ectodomain. The protein transits the PM during its normal lifecycle; the TM precursor is the source of the soluble form. This dual-form biology (TM precursor + shed soluble ligand) places EREG in the `dual_localization` / ectodomain-shedding bucket: the membrane-anchored stage is real but transient, and the soluble shed form is the dominant biological actor in most contexts. Surface accessibility exists via the TM-precursor stage.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Whether the membrane-anchored TM-precursor stage of EREG is documented with sufficient residency / stability on the cell surface to qualify as a canonical surface form, or whether the precursor is so rapidly cleaved that it functions primarily as a transient intermediate to the soluble ligand. EGF-family ligands typically show both juxtacrine (TM-precursor) and endocrine (shed soluble) signaling, but quantitative surface residence of the EREG precursor specifically is not detailed in the canonical literature summary provided.
